solution.p: We will factor t^{2}+9t+20 where a=1, b=9, and c=20.
Mental Method: When we factor a polynomial in the form of x^2+bx+c, x^2+bxy+cy^2, or x^2y^2+bxy+c we can use mental math to factor. We need to find two numbers whose product is c, and whose sum is b.
For t^{2}+9t+20, since the product of 5 and 4 is 20, and the sum of 5 and 4 is 9, the factorization is \begin{aligned}[t]t^{2}+9t+20\amp =\left(t+5\right)\mathopen{}\left(t+4\right)\end{aligned}
AC Method: In this method, we first calculate the product of a and c in ax^2+bx+c. For this problem, we have (1) \cdot (20) = 20.

The number 20 can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways:  egin{array}{lll}1(20)\amp -1(-20)\amp 2(10)\amp -2(-10)
ewline4(5)\amp -4(-5)\amp \end{array} Looking at the corresponding sums, we want a sum of 9, and this sum happens with 4+5=9 So we write  egin{aligned}[t]t^{2}+9t+20\amp =t^{2}+4t+5t+20
ewline \amp =t\mathopen{}\left(t+4ight)+5\mathopen{}\left(t+4ight)
ewline \amp =\left(t+5ight)\mathopen{}\left(t+4ight)\end{aligned}Generic Rectangles Method: First, we set up generic rectangles by putting the first term and third term in t^{2}+9t+20 into the top left and bottom right rectangles:
The first term's coefficient, 1, can be factored into the product of two numbers (where the first factor is positive) in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(1)\amp \end{array}
The third term, 20, can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(20)\amp -1(-20)\amp 2(10)\amp -2(-10)\newline4(5)\amp -4(-5)\amp \end{array}

We put each pair into the corresponding places next to those generic rectangles, and try to match the area of those rectangles with t^{2}+9t+20 by guess-and-check. The following generic rectangles show the solution:
Dimensions of those generic rectangles in the diagram give us the solution: \begin{aligned}[t]t^{2}+9t+20\amp =\left(t+5\right)\mathopen{}\left(t+4\right)\end{aligned}
